course_description:
In this course, students will examine how living things, from the building blocks of cells and the molecules that underpin them, to diverse organisms and ecosystems, persist and thrive by continuously responding to change. Through an integrated journey spanning biochemistry, cell structure, genetics, energy transformations, and evolutionary history, the course reveals how biological systems sense, adapt, and evolve. The narrative weaves together fundamental cellular mechanisms and the origins of biodiversity. By recognizing evolutionary innovations and physiological strategies, students will understand life’s remarkable flexibility in the face of shifting environments. This multidisciplinary approach highlights both the unity and diversity of life, underscoring the importance of adaptation as a central theme in biology.

Students may complete BIOL1000 and BIOL1020 concurrently, or BIOL1000 prior to completing BIOL1020, which is recommended.

This course explores how living systems, from molecules and cells to organisms and ecosystems, persist and evolve by responding to change. Through an integrated study of biochemistry, cell biology, genetics, energy transformations, and evolution, students will uncover how biological systems sense, adapt, and diversify. Emphasizing adaptation as a unifying theme, the course highlights life’s resilience and the connections between molecular mechanisms and biodiversity.

course_aims: The aim of this course is to develop students’ understanding of how living systems persist, adapt, and evolve in response to change. Through an integrated exploration of biochemistry, cell biology, genetics, ecology and evolution, students will appreciate the unity and diversity of life, and the central role of adaptation in shaping biological function and biodiversity. In parallel, the course fosters broader biological skills, including effective written and oral communication, problem solving, critical thinking, data interpretation and analysis, and the capacity to engage as AI-ready graduates in a rapidly evolving scientific landscape

learning_outcomes.description: Explain the structure and function of cells, including organelles and macromolecules, as foundations of life.
Describe energy flow in biological systems, including photosynthesis and cellular respiration.
Summarise principles of genetics, inheritance, and reproduction in living organisms.
Outline the diversity of life and evolutionary principles underpinning biological classification.
Describe core ecological concepts, including organism-environment interactions, energy transfer, and ecosystem dynamics.
Critically analyse and interpret biological data using digital and visual tools, including the ethical use of Artificial Intelligence (AI), to evaluate scientific evidence.

Recognise how biological knowledge, including Indigenous knowledges and perspectives of Country, informs debates on conservation, environment, and human impacts, fostering inclusive and culturally aware understanding.

assessment_summary.title: Inquiry and Synthesis in the AI Era - Part 1: Written
Inquiry and Synthesis in the AI Era – Part 2: In-Class assessment

assessment_details.title: Weekly Quizzes
assessment_details.due_date: Quiz 1: 5/03/2026 Quiz 2: 12/03/2026 Quiz 3: 19/03/2026 Quiz 4: 2/04/2026 Quiz 5: 16/04/2026 Quiz 6: 23/04/2026 Quiz 7: 14/05/2026 Quiz 8: 21/05/2026 Quiz 9: 28/05/2026 Due during your allocated Thursday Workshop.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Over the course of the semester, you will complete 9 x 10 minute multiple choice questionnaires. You will complete one quiz per week during workshop time in all weeks EXCEPT weeks 1, 5, 9, 10. These quizzes will be at start of your workshop, and we will take the best SIX out of the NINE quizzes to calculate your final grade for this assessment. Each quiz is worth 5%. Each quiz will target the course content from the week before. So for example, in week 2, you will be tested on your knowledge and comprehension of week 1. These quizzes will also serve as valuable revisions for your end of semester exam and indicate content areas where perhaps you need more guidance and support. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You will complete the quiz under exam conditions. The quiz will be accessed through the BIOL1000 Blackboard Ultra website. To access the quiz, you will be provided a password. The test will only be open for 15 min during the first 15 min of class time.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Extensions are not available for this assessment as they are paced to match course content. You need to complete SIX out the offered NINE quizzes to complete the assessment. If you complete more that SIX - the best six marks will be used to calculate your grade.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
If you are more than 5 minutes late to the workshop OR miss the workshop, then you will not be able to take the quiz. However, there are NINE opportunities to complete the quizzes and you only need to take SIX to complete the assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Inquiry and Synthesis in the AI Era - Part 1: Written
assessment_details.task_description: Part 1 of this assessment is a written component. This assessment develops your ability to locate, evaluate, and synthesise scientific literature using AI tools. You will be given a topic, build a focused reference list, justify your source choices, summarize key papers, and demonstrate understanding through in-class responses. AI tools are integrated to support, but not replace , your critical thinking and writing. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You submit your link through a Turnitin link accessed through your BIOL1000 course Blackboard site
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date. For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 4
learning_activities.topic: Lectures (Module 1) Each week you will attend a 2 hour interactive lecture. Module 1 = Responding to change in the cellular environment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 4
learning_activities.topic: Workshops (Module 1) Each week you will also attend a 2 hour workshop where you will be guided through activities related to the corresponding week's lecture. These workshops have been designed to help you understand key concepts. In week 1, you will be given a workbook, which you are expected to fill out during your workshop time. These workbooks are not graded, but will be an excellent revision guide for the final exam (the course hurdle). Hence, the more work and detail you put into your workbook, the more detailed revision guide you will have! In week 1 we will also discuss how to start work on your AI assessment. Weeks 2,3,4 will start with your MCQ quiz. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 5 To Week 8
learning_activities.topic: Lectures (Module 2) This lecture is the start of Module 2 - Responding to Change in the Natural Environment. This module will be lead by Dr. Nicola Browne. Learning outcomes: L05, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 5 To Week 8
learning_activities.topic: Workshops (Module 2) Over these four weeks, you will complete three MCQs at the start of workshops 6, 7 and 8. In week 5, you will also be provided time to work on your AI assessment and seek feedback on your progress. Learning outcomes: L05, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 12
learning_activities.topic: Lectures (Module 3) This week is the start of Module 3 = Responding to change in the evolutionary environment. Module 3 is led by Ass. Prof Anthony Young. Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 12
learning_activities.topic: Workshops (Module 3) Over these four weeks, you will complete three MCQs at the start of workshops 10, 11 and 12. In week 9, you will also be provided time to work on your AI assessment and seek feedback on your progress. Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L06
Revision lecture Revision lecture Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L07

Final workshop In this final workshop you will complete part of your AI assessment. There will also be time for reflection on your workbooks and feedback on how to improve your workbooks to enhance this revision tool for your end of semester exam. Learning outcomes: L06, L07
